elements.sent-2411-001: Deep neural networks, while achieving remarkable success across diverse tasks, demand significant resources, including computation, GPU memory, bandwidth, storage, and energy.
elements.sent-2411-002: Network quantization, as a standard compression and acceleration technique, reduces storage costs and enables potential inference acceleration by discretizing network weights and activations into a finite set of integer values.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, current quantization methods are often complex and sensitive, requiring extensive task-specific hyperparameters, where even a single misconfiguration can impair model performance, limiting generality across different models and tasks.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we propose Quantization without Tears (QwT), a method that simultaneously achieves quantization speed, accuracy, simplicity, and generality.
elements.sent-2411-005: The key insight of QwT is to incorporate a lightweight additional structure into the quantized network to mitigate information loss during quantization.
elements.sent-2411-006: This structure consists solely of a small set of linear layers, keeping the method simple and efficient.
elements.sent-2411-007: More importantly, it provides a closed-form solution, allowing us to improve accuracy effortlessly under 2 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-008: Extensive experiments across various vision, language, and multimodal tasks demonstrate that QwT is both highly effective and versatile.
elements.sent-2411-009: In fact, our approach offers a robust solution for network quantization that combines simplicity, accuracy, and adaptability, which provides new insights for the design of novel quantization paradigms.
elements.sent-2411-010: Along with their extraordinary breakthroughs in various vision~\cite{mask_rcnn , language~\cite{bert and multimodal~\cite{CLIP tasks, deep neural networks~\cite{resnet,vit also exhibit ferocious greed for various resources: compute, GPU memory, bandwidth, storage, energy, \etc. Hence, compressing and accelerating deep nets have not only attracted interests in academia, but are also an urgent need in real-world deployments and applications.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Among various research efforts in this direction, network quantization~\cite{quantization_survey is arguably the most practical one.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Different from unstructured pruning~\cite{unstructured_pruning , it is well supported by existing hardware.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Compared to structured pruning~\cite{li2016pruning,channel_pruning , its compression ratio is higher and its loss is relatively smaller.}
elements.sent-2411-014: For example, the INT8 quantization of both FP32 weights and activations leads to roughly 4$\times$ reduction in network size and 4$\times$ speedup with almost zero accuracy loss in many applications~\cite{i-vit , which far exceeds structured pruning.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Existing methods are often categorized as Post-Training Quantization (PTQ)~\cite{CL-Calib,PD-Quant,IGQ-ViT,RepQ-ViT,PTQ4ViT,FQ-ViT or Quantization-Aware Training (QAT)~\cite{ReactNet,ewgs,LSQ,Q-ViT,zhu2023quantized , where the difference is whether training is required (`no' for PTQ and `yes' for QAT).}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Quantization, however, is not as perfect as it seems to be.
elements.sent-2411-017: There are also obvious drawbacks and pitfalls in existing quantization methods.
elements.sent-2411-018: The speed-accuracy dilemma: PTQ can be thousands of times faster than QAT during the quantization process, but QAT may well be 10 percentage points higher than PTQ in accuracy during inference.
elements.sent-2411-019: Complexity: Quantization methods are often delicate and tricky.
elements.sent-2411-020: They often have tons of hyperparameters to tune for each specific task, and even one improperly set hyperparameter value may ruin the quantized model.
elements.sent-2411-021: Missing generality: Relevant to their complexities, an existing method is often geared toward a specific model and/or task.
elements.sent-2411-022: Different models/tasks require different quantization methods.
elements.sent-2411-023: Given the status quo, at this moment it does not seem unreasonable to treat the act of network quantization as an art rather than an established engineering tool.
elements.sent-2411-024: In this paper, we propose a Quantization without Tears (QwT) method to address these drawbacks, which achieves quantization speed, accuracy, simplicity and generality simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-025: The key to achieve these goals simultaneously is to slightly change the quantization paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-026: Suppose a network $M$ has the network structure $S$ and parameters $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-027: Current quantization methods will quantize it into a model $M^{\mathbb Z}$ with the same structure $S$ $(S^{\mathbb Z =S)$ and quantized parameters $\theta^{\mathbb Z}$ in the integer format.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Our key argument is that the quantized structure does not need to be strictly $S$.
elements.sent-2411-029: In our QwT, it becomes $S \cup S_c$, where some extra modules $S_c$ are added to the network structure to compensate for the information loss due to quantized parameters and activations.
elements.sent-2411-030: The extra $S_c$ thus help us achieve high accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-031: But, it is also obvious that so long as the size and computation of $S_c$ is small or even negligible when compared to $S^{\mathbb Z}$, we achieve both speed and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-032: In our QwT, $S_c$ has very simple structures: only few linear layers, which renders it both simple and general.
elements.sent-2411-033: To be more specific, the parameters in $S_c$ can be set in closed-form with a small calibration set, which in almost all cases leads to significantly higher accuracy than PTQ methods.
elements.sent-2411-034: To sum up, the contributions of this paper are:.
elements.sent-2411-035: Proposing a new paradigm for network quantization by lifting the restriction that the quantized network structure $S^{\mathbb Z}$ has to be exactly the same as that of the original network structure $S$.
elements.sent-2411-036: Proposing QwT, a simple and general quantization method without tears in this new paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-037: QwT achieves speed, accuracy, simplicity and generality simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-038: Extensive experiments have been carried out, which show that QwT has the following properties:.
elements.sent-2411-040: For example, QwT quantizes a ViT network in roughly 2 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-041: During inference, its throughput is almost the same as models quantized by existing quantization methods.
elements.sent-2411-042: QwT is significantly more accurate than existing PTQ methods even without any back-propagation.
elements.sent-2411-043: On top of that, if higher accuracy is requested, QwT requires only 1 epoch of training to approach the accuracy of QAT methods.
elements.sent-2411-044: In contrast, QAT often requires a large number of epochs (\eg, 200 epochs).
elements.sent-2411-045: There is zero (0) hyperparameters to tune, and the parameters in $S_c$ can be found in closed-form.
elements.sent-2411-046: Exactly the same QwT method has been successfully applied to various networks and applications, including both CNNs~\cite{resnet and Transformers (ViT~\cite{vit and Swin~\cite{swin ), object recognition, detection (with both Mask R-CNN~\cite{mask_rcnn and DETR~\cite{DETR ) and segmentation, multimodal models (CLIP~\cite{CLIP ), generative models (DiT~\cite{dit ), and large language models (LLaMA~\cite{dubey2024llama3herdmodels ).}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: In addition to quantizing to low-bits in simulators, the same QwT method can quantize a model that is able to run directly on GPUs with minimum efforts (\ie, quantization `without tears'): Simply obtain $\theta^{\mathbb Z}$ using TensorRT, then add $S_c$ using QwT.
elements.sent-2411-048: The resulting quantized model is then ready to be deployed on GPUs that support quantized fix-point inference.
elements.sent-2411-049: Network quantization~\cite{quantization_survey aims to reduce the bit-width of weights and activations, enabling the quantized model to be stored more efficiently and to perform faster inference with suitable hardware support.}
elements.sent-2411-050: The fundamental principle of network quantization involves approximating full-precision weights and activations by mapping them to a finite set of discrete values, which are subsequently used in forward model computations (\ie, in inference).
elements.sent-2411-051: One line of research focuses on quantization-aware training (QAT)~\cite{ReactNet,ewgs,LSQ,Q-ViT , which integrates quantization into the training process using back-propagation, where the straight-through estimator~\cite{ste is commonly employed to approximate gradients for non-differentiable rounding functions.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Another line of research concentrates on post-training quantization (PTQ)~\cite{PTQ4ViT,gptq,RepQ-ViT,FQ-ViT , which converts a fully trained full-precision model into low-bit format using a small set of calibration samples.}
elements.sent-2411-053: AdaRound~\cite{adaround proposed an adaptive weight-rounding mechanism.}
elements.sent-2411-054: BRECQ~\cite{brecq leveraged block reconstruction for quantization, utilizing the Fisher Information Matrix to guide the process.}
elements.sent-2411-055: QDrop~\cite{qdrop randomly dropped the quantization of activations during quantization to achieve flatness of the low-bit model.}
elements.sent-2411-056: While these methods~\cite{adaround,brecq,qdrop have proven effective on ResNet~\cite{resnet backbones, applying them directly to ViT~\cite{vit often degrades recognition accuracy since the intrinsic structure of the softmax attention is incompatible with these methods.}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: This poses new challenges to design general PTQ methods for the Transformer architecture.
elements.sent-2411-058: To address this issue,~\cite{rank_loss introduced a ranking loss designed to preserve the relative order between quantized and non-quantized attention scores.}
elements.sent-2411-059: PTQ4ViT~\cite{PTQ4ViT proposed twin uniform quantization for shifted activations and a Hessian-guided metric to generate scaling factors.}
elements.sent-2411-060: RepQ-ViT~\cite{RepQ-ViT decoupled quantization and inference, employing distinct quantizers to enable precise quantization while simultaneously ensuring efficient inference.}
elements.sent-2411-061: IGQ-ViT~\cite{IGQ-ViT introduced instance-aware group quantization for ViT to dynamically allocate channels of activation maps to different quantization groups.}
elements.sent-2411-062: GPTQ~\cite{gptq introduced a one-shot weight quantization technique that exploits approximate second-order information.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Different from all of these methods, we propose a new quantization paradigm that introduces a lightweight module to compensate for the information loss caused by quantization.
elements.sent-2411-064: This new paradigm allows our method to be seamlessly integrated with any state-of-the-art quantization methods as a plugin in a completely black-box fashion.
elements.sent-2411-065: Experiments demonstrate that our method is highly compatible with various PTQ approaches, enabling effortless improvements in recognition accuracy within just 2 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-066: We start by outlining key concepts and notations related to network quantization.
elements.sent-2411-067: Given a quantization bit-width $b$, the quantization function $Q(\cdot | b): \mathbb{R \rightarrow \mathbb{Z}$ maps a floating-point number $x$ (\eg, weight or activation) into its corresponding fixed-point representation $x^{\mathbb{Z }$ encoded by $b$ bits.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Among various quantization approaches, uniform quantization is particularly favored thanks to its simplicity and compatibility with hardware deployment.
elements.sent-2411-069: The uniform quantization procedure is formalized as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:uniform_quant} x^{\mathbb{Z}} = \text{clip}\left( \nint{\frac{x}{s}} + z, 0, 2^b - 1 \right) \,,\end{equation} in which $s \in \mathbb{R ^+$ represents the quantization scale, and $z \in \mathbb{Z}$ denotes the zero-point offset.}
elements.sent-2411-070: These parameters are determined as follows: \begin{equation}s = \frac{\max(x) - \min(x)}{2^b - 1} \,, \label{s} z = clip\left( \nint{-\frac{\min(x)}{s}}, 0, 2^b - 1 \right) \,. \label{z}\end{equation} In these equations, $\nint{.}$ denotes the rounding function, and the $\text{clip (\cdot, a, b)$ operation constrains the input value into the range $[a, b]$.}
elements.sent-2411-071: The reconstructed quantized output can then be formulated as: \begin{equation}\hat{x} = s \times (x^{\mathbb{Z}} - z) \,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-072: Beyond the naive uniform quantizer, a range of more sophisticated quantization techniques~\cite{AdaLog,adaround,PTQ4ViT,RepQ-ViT have been proposed and extensively studied by the community.}
elements.sent-2411-073: In the literature, quantization methods typically quantize both the model weights and activations.
elements.sent-2411-074: Quantization significantly reduces the storage requirements by enabling models to be stored in lower bit formats.
elements.sent-2411-075: Additionally, thanks to hardware support for integer-only computations, operations involving the quantized representations $x^{\mathbb{Z }$, such as matrix multiplications between quantized weights and activations, can be performed with substantial improvements in computational efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-076: But, obviously there is significant information loss between $x$ and $x^{\mathbb{Z }$, and it grows very fast when many layers of computation and quantization are stacked together.}
elements.sent-2411-077: To recover from the resulting accuracy loss, QAT methods resort to many epochs of training, which leads to the speed-accuracy dilemma and complex, ad hoc quantization methods.
elements.sent-2411-078: Given a model $M=(S,\theta)$, where $S$ and $\theta$ denote its structure and weights, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-079: Existing quantization techniques transform $M$ into a quantized version $M^{\mathbb Z =(S^{\mathbb Z ,\theta^{\mathbb Z )$, which maintains the same network structure (\ie, $S=S^{\mathbb Z}$) while modifying the original parameters $\theta$ to their quantized counterparts $\theta^{\mathbb Z}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Our key insight is to challenge this structural rigidity: the quantized model does not necessarily need to retain the exactly same structural configuration, \ie, it is legitimate to allow $S^{\mathbb Z \neq S$.}
elements.sent-2411-081: We argue that some extra modules $S_c$ can be added to the quantized model, such that its structure $S^{\mathbb{Z =S \cup S_c$.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: The extra modules in $S_c$ can compensate for the information loss caused by quantization.
elements.sent-2411-083: More specifically, modern deep nets typically compose of many blocks, \eg, bottleneck blocks in ResNet~\cite{resnet or Transformer blocks in ViT~\cite{vit .}}
elements.sent-2411-084: Let $l_i$ denote the $i$-th block in a model, and let $x_i \in \mathbb R^{d_{in }$ and $y_i \in \mathbb{R ^{d_{out }$ be the input and output of this block $l_i$, respectively, such that $y_i = l_i(x_i)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: We argue that we can add a compensation module $c_i$ for this block.
elements.sent-2411-087: For notational simplicity, we omit the subscript $i$ from now on, \ie, we represent a block as $y=l(x)$ and the compensation module is simply denoted as $c$.
elements.sent-2411-088: After quantization, the input activations and weights of the block $l$ are modified into the quantized version $x^{\mathbb{Z }$ and $l^{\mathbb{Z }$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-090: Clearly, there is information loss in all 3 quantization pairs: $l \mapsto l^{\mathbb{Z }$, $x \mapsto x^{\mathbb{Z }$ and $y \mapsto y^{\mathbb{Z }$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: What is intriguing is that the information loss is obviously highly non-linear in all 3 pairs.
elements.sent-2411-092: To implement the compensation idea, we have to answer the following questions:.
elements.sent-2411-093: How to measure the information losses in all 3 pairs that interplay with each other in a complex manner?.
elements.sent-2411-094: How to design the compensation module $c$ that accounts for these highly non-linear information losses?.
elements.sent-2411-095: We propose a QwT (quantization without tears) method, which answers both questions in the simplest possible form.
elements.sent-2411-096: First, the information loss is measured by $\|y-y^{\mathbb{Z }\|^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Because $y$ is the output of $l$, it naturally takes care of information losses in $x^{\mathbb{Z }$ and $l^{\mathbb{Z }$---when $y^{\mathbb{Z =y$, intuitively there is absolutely zero information loss even if $x \neq x^{\mathbb{Z }$ and $l \neq l^{\mathbb{Z }$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Note that $\|y-y^{\mathbb{Z }\|^2$ also accounts for cumulative information losses.}
elements.sent-2411-099: That is, in the $i$-th block, $c_i$ compensates information losses accumulated in all previous blocks that have not yet been corrected by $c_1, c_2, \dots, c_{i-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-100: Second, because of this cumulative nature of our choice, in QwT we choose to implement $c$ (index $i$ omitted) as a simple linear layer.
elements.sent-2411-101: Although it is impossible to accurately compensate the non-linear information loss in one block via a linear layer, we have many chances to repeatedly apply linear corrections.
elements.sent-2411-102: The entire compensation formed by all extra modules is in fact non-linear because it interacts with the quantized network in every block.
elements.sent-2411-103: To be concrete, we define $c(x)=Wx+b$ and then \begin{equation}\label{eq:qwt} y^{\text{QwT}} = l^{\mathbb{Z}} (x^{\mathbb{Z}}) + c(x^{\mathbb{Z}}) \,,\end{equation} where $W \in \mathbb{R ^{ d_{out \times d_{in }$ and $b \in \mathbb{R ^{d_{out }$ are the weight matrix and bias vector of the linear layer $c$, respectively.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-104: The QwT structure is illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:qwt .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.9\columnwidth]{figs/QwT_illustration.pdf} \caption{Illustration of QwT in one block. QwT adds a simple linear layer to any model block, compensating for information loss using the block input $x^{\mathbb{Z}}$. This approach is straightforward and compatible with almost all types of backbones~\cite{vit,swin,resnet}.} \label{fig:qwt}
elements.sent-2411-106: They not only make QwT conceptually the simplest, but also ensures a close-form solution.
elements.sent-2411-107: To minimize the difference between $y$ and $y^{\mathbb{Z }$, we select a small set of training examples (512 images) from the training set.}
elements.sent-2411-108: Using these samples, QwT collects all inputs for the block $l^{\mathbb{Z }$ to form a matrix $X^{\mathbb{Z \in \mathbb{R ^{d_{in \times N}$, where $N$ is the total number of features or tokens.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: We feed $X^{\mathbb{Z }$ into the quantized block $l^{\mathbb{Z }$ to obtain $Y^{\mathbb{Z \in \mathbb{R ^{d_{out \times N}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: Next, We feed $X^{\mathbb{Z }$ into the non-quantized block $l$ to obtain $Y \in \mathbb{R ^{d_{out \times N}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: Correspondingly, $Y$ contains output of the matching block in the original model.
elements.sent-2411-112: Our task is then to estimate $Y-Y^{\mathbb{Z}$ using $X^{\mathbb{Z }$}.}
elements.sent-2411-113: This is a classic linear regression problem, and has a closed-form solution: \begin{equation}W^* = (Y-Y^{\mathbb{Z}}) {X^{\mathbb{Z}}}^\top (X^{\mathbb{Z}} {X^{\mathbb{Z}}}^\top)^{-1} \,. \label{eq:W_star}\end{equation} In Equation~\ref{eq:W_star , to simplify the solution, the bias $b$ is absorbed into $W$ and a row vector of $\mathbf{1 ^\top$ are concatenated to $X^{\mathbb Z}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-114: It is worth mentioning that theoretically QwT will not make the quantized network getting worse---by setting $W$ and $b$ to all zeros, QwT will not alter the quantized network.
elements.sent-2411-115: Note that after the QwT module for $l^{\mathbb{Z }$ is inserted, the compensation in the next block depends on all the previous QwT modules.}
elements.sent-2411-116: Consequently, the information loss from $y$ to $y^{\mathbb{Z }$ is gradually compensated block by block, allowing $c$ to account for the accumulated loss from all preceding blocks that remain uncorrected.}
elements.sent-2411-117: In our experiments, we observed that the coefficient of determination ($R^2$)~\cite{for_r2 for a small subset ($<$5\%) of QwT modules was notably low, adversely hurting recognition accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Consequently, we apply the initialization using Equation~\ref{eq:W_star only when $R^2>0$; Otherwise, the $W$ and $b$ of the QwT module are set to zero.}
elements.sent-2411-119: Finally, the QwT method has a simple pipeline: first quantize a model $M$ using any PTQ method, then add the compensation module $c_i$ to every block $i$ and set the parameters in $c_i$ using Equation~\ref{eq:W_star .}
elements.sent-2411-120: A notable advantage of QwT lies in its inherent simplicity.
elements.sent-2411-121: This simplicity ensures that the initialization process of QwT modules is highly efficient, which requires roughly 2 minutes in practice to compensate for the information loss during quantization, thereby enhancing recognition accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-122: Experimental results demonstrated that QwT exhibits significant versatility and efficiency across various vision and language tasks.

elements.sent-2411-135: The results show that incorporating the QwT module consistently boosts the recognition accuracy, leading to an average increase of approximately 2.6\%, and even up to 5\% for 4-bit quantization, highlighting that QwT is particularly effective for low-bit scenarios.

elements.sent-2411-228: In this paper, we proposed Quantization without Tears (QwT), a novel approach that incorporates a lightweight structure into quantized models to compensate for the information loss during network quantization.
elements.sent-2411-229: The QwT modules, implemented as a tiny set of linear layers and seamlessly integrated into backbone blocks, achieved accuracy, simplicity, and generality simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-230: Notably, QwT provides a closed-form solution to complete the compensation process in under 2 minutes and enables effortless integration with existing quantization techniques.
elements.sent-2411-231: Extensive experiments demonstrated QwT’s exceptional effectiveness and versatility across a wide range of tasks, models, and quantization methods, advancing a streamlined and flexible paradigm for network quantization.

elements.sent-2411-265: In the originally published camera-ready version of \href{https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2025/papers/Fu_Quantization_without_Tears_CVPR_2025_paper.pdf {this paper , an issue in the implementation of the Percentile PTQ baseline led to incorrect configuration labels in Table~\ref{tab:imagenet for the ResNet-50 backbone.}}}
elements.sent-2411-266: The results reported under ``4/4'' and ``6/6'' bit-width settings were actually obtained using ``4/8'' and ``6/8'' configurations, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-267: We have now corrected Table~\ref{tab:imagenet to display the right ``4/4'' and ``6/6'' results.}
elements.sent-2411-268: Furthermore, in Table~\ref{tab:resnet , we have extended our experiments to include all weight and activation bit-width combinations (``4/4'', ``4/8'', ``6/6'', and ``6/8'') for completeness.}
elements.sent-2411-269: These updates do not affect any of the paper's conclusions.
elements.sent-2411-270: We apologize for any confusion caused.
